disease.name: Ischemic Colitis
disease.note: Most common form of GI ischemia from acute colonic hypoperfusion. Watershed areas: splenic flexure (Griffith point) and rectosigmoid junction (Sudeck point). Sudden onset crampy LLQ pain + urgent bloody diarrhea. 80% resolve spontaneously. Must differentiate from gangrenous form requiring emergency surgery.

severityAssessment.severityLabel: Peritonitis/gangrene/sepsis -> emergency surgery; no peritonitis -> supportive care, 80% resolve

subtypes.name: Stricturing Ischemic Colitis
subtypes.notes: Deeper injury, fibrotic stricture over weeks-months

firstLine.test: CT abdomen with IV contrast (colonic wall thickening, pneumatosis)
Stool culture and C. diff toxin

secondLine.test: Colonoscopy (if no peritonitis - patchy erythema, ulceration, hemorrhagic nodules at watershed)
Mesenteric angiography (if SMA territory or pain out of proportion)

investigations.goldStandard: CT abdomen with IV contrast showing segmental colonic wall thickening at watershed areas with pericolonic stranding

emergency: Emergency laparotomy if peritonitis/pneumatosis/hemodynamic instability - Hartmann procedure preferred
IV broad-spectrum antibiotics (gram-negative and anaerobic coverage)
inpatient: Correct underlying cause (optimize cardiac output, discontinue vasoconstrictors)
Supportive care for transient form - 80% resolve in 1-2 weeks
Serial abdominal exams and lactate monitoring
outpatient: Colonoscopy in 6-8 weeks after resolution to document healing
Low-dose aspirin if cardiac indication not contraindicated
refractory: Elective surgical resection for stricturing type
Endoscopic dilation for short benign strictures

disease.pathophysiology: Acute decrease in colonic blood flow (non-occlusive: low flow/hypotension, or occlusive: emboli/thrombus) → hypoxic injury to colonic mucosa (most sensitive layer) → mucosal edema and hemorrhage → inflammatory infiltration → superficial mucosal necrosis → ulceration → hematochezia. Watershed areas (splenic flexure, rectosigmoid) most vulnerable due to tenuous collateral circulation between SMA and IMA territories.
mustNotMiss: Differentiate transient (80%) from gangrenous (8%) - peritonitis/pneumatosis mandates emergency surgery
Pain out of proportion to exam suggests SMA occlusion (acute mesenteric ischemia) not ischemic colitis
Do NOT scope if peritonitis or pneumatosis - risk of perforation
Colon cancer can mimic ischemic stricture - biopsy mandatory

diagnosticClues.clue: Sudden LLQ pain + urgent bloody diarrhea in elderly with vascular disease
Segmental colitis at watershed areas (splenic flexure, rectosigmoid) with rectal sparing on colonoscopy
Colonic wall thickening with pericolonic stranding at watershed on CT

exclusionClues.clue: Positive stool culture or C. diff toxin (infectious colitis)
Inflamed diverticula with abscess on CT (diverticulitis)
